edges.description: Arsenic metabolites generate reactive oxygen species
Arsenic alters DNA methylation patterns globally
Arsenic and its metabolites accumulating in skin directly perturb keratinocyte and melanocyte homeostasis, independent of malignant transformation.
Reactive oxygen species cause oxidative base lesions and DNA strand breaks
Arsenic inhibits DNA repair enzyme activity
Accumulated DNA damage leads to chromosomal abnormalities
Tumor suppressor pathways including p53 become dysregulated
Repair deficiency allows accumulation of mutations
Loss of checkpoint control enables proliferation
Accumulated mutations in growth regulatory genes
Transformed, uncontrolled proliferation of epidermal keratinocytes produces arsenic-induced squamous and basal cell skin cancers.
Uncontrolled proliferation of transformed respiratory epithelium produces arsenic-associated lung cancer, typically after a 20-40 year latency.
Uncontrolled proliferation of transformed urothelium produces arsenic-associated urinary bladder cancer.
Arsenic-driven keratinocyte hyperproliferation and altered differentiation produce punctate/nodular arsenical keratoses on the palms and soles.
Arsenic-induced melanocyte dysregulation produces the characteristic "raindrop" pattern of hyperpigmentation with interspersed hypopigmented macules.

nodes.description: Inorganic arsenic is absorbed from the gut or lungs and undergoes hepatic methylation by arsenic (+3 oxidation state) methyltransferase (AS3MT). The metabolic pathway produces monomethylarsonic acid (MMA) and dimethylarsinic acid (DMA). The trivalent intermediate MMA(III) is highly reactive and toxic. Genetic polymorphisms in AS3MT affect methylation efficiency and cancer risk.

evidence.reference_title: Arsenic exposure in Latin America: biomarkers, risk assessments and related health effects.
evidence.snippet: Once within hepatocytes, arsenite (+III) methyltransferase (AS3MT) facilitates conversion to methylarsonic acid (MMAV).

nodes.description: Independent of frank malignant transformation, arsenic accumulating in the keratin-rich epidermis perturbs keratinocyte growth and differentiation and melanocyte pigmentation through altered growth-factor signaling and oxidative stress. This produces the non-malignant hallmark skin signs of chronic arsenicosis—palmoplantar arsenical keratoses and "raindrop" hyperpigmentation—which characteristically precede and flank arsenic-related skin cancers.

evidence.snippet: Skin hyperpigmentation and hyperkeratosis have long been known to be the hallmark signs of chronic As exposure.
Oxidative stress, chromosomal abnormality and altered growth factors are possible modes of action in arsenic carcinogenesis.

nodes.description: Transitional cell carcinoma of the urinary bladder. One of the most strongly associated arsenic-related internal malignancies. Latency period of 20-40 years.

evidence.snippet: Long-term exposure to inorganic arsenic (As) from drinking water has been documented to induce cancers in lung, urinary bladder, kidney, liver and skin in a dose-response relationship.

nodes.description: Arsenic causes global DNA hypomethylation while inducing promoter-specific hypermethylation of tumor suppressor genes. This occurs partly through competition for methyl groups (arsenic methylation depletes SAM) and altered DNMT activity. Histone modifications and miRNA expression are also affected, contributing to aberrant gene expression patterns.

evidence.reference_title: Epimutagenesis: A prospective mechanism to remediate arsenic-induced toxicity.
evidence.snippet: SAM dictates methylation status of the genome and arsenic metabolism leads to depletion of SAM leading to an epigenetic disequilibrium.

nodes.description: Elimination of arsenic exposure is the primary intervention. Providing clean water through wells drilled to arsenic-free aquifers, water treatment systems, or alternative sources dramatically reduces ongoing exposure and may stabilize premalignant lesions.

The combination of oxidative DNA damage, impaired repair, and aberrant mitotic spindle function leads to genomic instability, aneuploidy, and chromosomal aberrations. Arsenic disrupts spindle assembly by targeting tubulin and centrosome function.

evidence.snippet: Arsenic exposure is associated with G2/M cell cycle arrest and DNA aneuploidy in both cultured keratinocytes

evidence.reference_title: Arsenic exposure in Latin America: biomarkers, risk assessments and related health effects.
evidence.snippet: Several studies in Latin America have shown that chromosomal aberrations were more frequent in a Mexican population with high exposure (0.39 mg/L of As in drinking water) compared to those with low exposure (0.019–0.026 mg/L)

nodes.description: Arsenic inhibits multiple DNA repair pathways including nucleotide excision repair, base excision repair, and mismatch repair. It inhibits zinc finger proteins involved in repair by displacing zinc. PARP-1, XPA, and other repair factors are direct targets. This repair deficiency synergizes with oxidative damage to promote mutagenesis.

evidence.reference_title: Arsenic exposure in Latin America: biomarkers, risk assessments and related health effects.
evidence.snippet: Enhancing any genotoxicity of As would be the impairment of DNA repair capabilities that was observed by Andrew et al. in Mexico

nodes.description: Increased risk of lung cancer, particularly squamous cell carcinoma. Synergistic with tobacco smoking. May occur after 20-40 year latency.

evidence.snippet: Long-term exposure to inorganic arsenic (As) from drinking water has been documented to induce cancers in lung, urinary bladder, kidney, liver and skin in a dose-response relationship.

nodes.description: Reactive oxygen species generated by arsenic metabolism produce oxidative DNA lesions including 8-oxoguanine, DNA strand breaks, and chromosomal aberrations. Unlike classic genotoxins, arsenic does not form direct DNA adducts but induces damage indirectly through this oxidative route.

evidence.reference_title: Arsenic exposure in Latin America: biomarkers, risk assessments and related health effects.
evidence.snippet: A follow-up study evaluated oxidative DNA damage, as indicated by cellular levels of 8-Oxo-2'-deoxyguanosine (8-oxo-dG).

nodes.description: Characteristic arsenic keratoses appearing as punctate or nodular hyperkeratotic lesions on palms and soles. Pathognomonic for chronic arsenic poisoning. May progress to squamous cell carcinoma.

evidence.snippet: Skin hyperpigmentation and hyperkeratosis have long been known to be the hallmark signs of chronic As exposure.

nodes.description: Arsenic and its trivalent methylated metabolites, notably the highly reactive MMA(III), generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) through mitochondrial dysfunction and NADPH oxidase activation. This oxidative burden, rather than direct DNA adduct formation, is the proximal insult that drives arsenic genotoxicity.

evidence.snippet: Oxidative stress, chromosomal abnormality and altered growth factors are possible modes of action in arsenic carcinogenesis.

evidence.reference_title: Arsenic exposure in Latin America: biomarkers, risk assessments and related health effects.
evidence.snippet: Arsenic is believed to have a broad array of detrimental effects that contribute to the development of acute and chronic disease states, including genotoxicity, oxidative stress, and altered DNA repair capacity

nodes.description: Multiple squamous cell carcinomas and basal cell carcinomas, often in sun-protected areas. May arise from keratotic lesions or Bowen disease (squamous carcinoma in situ). Bowen disease appears as well-demarcated erythematous plaques.

evidence.snippet: Long-term exposure to inorganic arsenic (As) from drinking water has been documented to induce cancers in lung, urinary bladder, kidney, liver and skin in a dose-response relationship.

nodes.description: Characteristic "raindrop" pattern of hyperpigmentation with interspersed hypopigmented macules, typically on the trunk. One of the earliest signs of chronic arsenicosis.

evidence.snippet: Skin hyperpigmentation and hyperkeratosis have long been known to be the hallmark signs of chronic As exposure.

nodes.description: Surgical removal of skin cancers and premalignant keratoses. May require multiple procedures given multiplicity of lesions. Mohs surgery for invasive carcinomas in cosmetically sensitive areas.

Standard treatment protocols for lung cancer (surgery, chemotherapy, immunotherapy, radiation) and bladder cancer (TURBT, intravesical therapy, cystectomy, systemic chemotherapy) based on stage and tumor characteristics.
Topical 5-fluorouracil or imiquimod for superficial lesions and field therapy of areas with multiple keratoses. Photodynamic therapy is also effective for widespread lesions.
Arsenic exposure perturbs tumor suppressor pathways, especially p53-linked checkpoint control, impairing growth control without requiring a recurrent coding mutation.

evidence.reference_title: Arsenic exposure in Latin America: biomarkers, risk assessments and related health effects.
evidence.snippet: Potentially as a result of As-induced DNA damage, levels of tumor suppressor protein p53 were elevated in high-exposure individuals

nodes.description: Combined effects of epigenetic dysregulation, tumor suppressor pathway dysfunction, genomic instability, and constitutive growth signaling drive proliferation of transformed cells in target organs. The skin, lung, and bladder are particularly susceptible due to their exposure to arsenic and its metabolites.

evidence.snippet: Long-term exposure to inorganic arsenic (As) from drinking water has been documented to induce cancers in lung, urinary bladder, kidney, liver and skin in a dose-response relationship.
